Source 1: Company Memo
GreenLeaf Inc. reported Q3 revenue of $4.2 million, a 15% increase from Q2. The CEO credited three new organic product lines launched in July. Employee headcount grew from 120 to 135. A second distribution center opened in Austin, Texas. Marketing expenses rose 8% from Q2.

Source 2: Financial Summary
GreenLeaf Quarterly Performance:
Q1: Revenue $3.1M, Employees 110, Marketing $280K
Q2: Revenue $3.65M, Employees 120, Marketing $310K
Q3: Revenue $4.2M, Employees 135, Marketing $335K
Q4 (Projected): Revenue $4.5M, Employees 140, Marketing $350K
questionStem: Based on the information provided, what was the approximate revenue per employee in Q3?
explanation: Q3 revenue $4.2M with 135 employees. $4,200,000/135 ≈ $31,111. Answer: C.

The Northeast region exceeded its August sales target by 12%. Hartford led with $890K, Boston $720K, New York $680K. Total regional sales: $2.29 million.

August 2024 Regional Sales Targets: Northeast $2.05M, Southeast $1.80M, Midwest $1.65M, West $2.30M. Total Company Target: $7.80M. Any region exceeding its target by more than 10% qualifies for the quarterly bonus pool.
questionStem: Does the information support the conclusion that the Northeast region qualifies for the quarterly bonus pool?

options.text: Yes, because the Northeast exceeded its target by 12%, which is more than the 10% threshold
Yes, because the Hartford office alone exceeded the regional target
No, because the 12% excess applies only to one office, not the whole region
No, because quarterly bonuses require exceeding targets in all three months
Cannot be determined from the information given

explanation: Source 1: Northeast exceeded target by 12%. Source 2: regions exceeding target by >10% qualify. 12%>10%, so Northeast qualifies. Answer: A.

Source 1: Product Review
TechVault CloudSync Pro launched in March with strong reviews. It meets AES-256 encryption standards, supports up to 50 simultaneous users (basic) and 200 (enterprise). Basic plan: $15/user/month. Reviewers noted the UI could improve but rated functionality highly.

Source 2: Competitor Comparison (Monthly per-user pricing)
CloudSync Pro: Basic $15, Enterprise $28
DataShield: Basic $18, Enterprise $32
VaultKeeper: Basic $12, Enterprise $25 (max 100 enterprise users)
SafeCloud: Basic $20, Enterprise $35
All products support AES-256 encryption.
questionStem: A company with 150 employees needing simultaneous access wants the most affordable enterprise plan with AES-256 encryption. Which product should they choose?
explanation: Need 150 simultaneous users. VaultKeeper max is 100 — eliminated. All have AES-256. Enterprise prices: CloudSync $28, DataShield $32, SafeCloud $35. CloudSync Pro is cheapest at $28. Answer: A.

passage:
Source 1: Supply Chain Report
AutoParts Inc. sources components from three suppliers: Alpha (40% of volume), Beta (35%), and Gamma (25%). Alpha is based in Germany, Beta in Mexico, and Gamma in Vietnam. Average lead times: Alpha — 21 days, Beta — 10 days, Gamma — 30 days. AutoParts maintains a safety stock of 14 days for all components. The company's monthly component consumption is 50,000 units.

Source 2: Disruption Alert
A major port strike in Hamburg, Germany began on March 1, 2025, and is expected to last 3-4 weeks. Shipping from German ports is completely halted. Alternative routing through Rotterdam adds 10 days to delivery. No disruptions have been reported at Mexican or Vietnamese ports.
questionStem: If AutoParts relies on the Rotterdam alternative for Alpha's shipments, approximately how many days of Alpha-sourced components will the company be short before the next delivery arrives?

options.text: No shortage — safety stock covers the delay

explanation: Alpha's normal lead time is 21 days. The Rotterdam alternative adds 10 days, making the new lead time 31 days. AutoParts has 14 days of safety stock. If a new order is placed via Rotterdam, it takes 31 days to arrive. The safety stock covers 14 of those days. Shortage period: 31 - 14 = 17 days.

All members receive access to weight rooms and cardio equipment. Group fitness classes require an additional $20/month add-on. Personal training sessions cost $60/session or $200 for a 4-session package. A 15% student discount applies to the base membership only, not to add-ons.

Source 2: Customer Inquiry
Name: Jordan Lee (college student)
Interested in: Annual membership with group fitness classes and a 4-session personal training package
Budget: $75/month plus a one-time $200 expense

No, his monthly costs would exceed his budget by approximately $2/month
No, his monthly costs would exceed his budget by approximately $7/month
Yes, the student discount brings his total monthly and one-time costs within budget

Monthly total: $33.15 + $20 = $53.15, well within his $75/month budget. The 4-session PT package is $200, which matches his one-time budget. Jordan can afford everything.

Source 1: Tax Bracket Information
For the 2024 tax year, the fictional state of Newland applies the following income tax brackets for single filers: First $20,000: 5%. $20,001 to $60,000: 8%. $60,001 to $120,000: 12%. Over $120,000: 15%. A standard deduction of $8,000 applies to all filers. Charitable donations above $500 are deductible up to a maximum of $5,000.

Source 2: Taxpayer Information
Filer: Nancy Owens
Filing Status: Single
Gross Income: $95,000
Charitable Donations: $3,200
Other Deductions: None
questionStem: What is Nancy's total Newland state income tax liability for 2024?
explanation: Gross income: $95,000. Standard deduction: $8,000. Charitable deduction: donations above $500 threshold = $3,200 - $500 = $2,700. Taxable income: $95,000 - $8,000 - $2,700 = $84,300. Tax calculation: First $20,000 × 5% = $1,000. Next $40,000 × 8% = $3,200. Remaining $24,300 × 12% = $2,916. Total tax: $1,000 + $3,200 + $2,916 = $7,116.

passage:
Source 1: Clinical Trial Report
A Phase III clinical trial for Drug ABX-200 enrolled 1,200 patients with moderate hypertension. The treatment group (600 patients) received ABX-200 daily for 12 weeks. The control group (600 patients) received a placebo. Results: Average blood pressure reduction in the treatment group was 18 mmHg systolic and 10 mmHg diastolic. The control group showed a 4 mmHg systolic and 2 mmHg diastolic reduction. Adverse effects in the treatment group: headache (12%), dizziness (8%), fatigue (5%).

Source 2: Regulatory Threshold
The drug regulatory authority requires a minimum 10 mmHg systolic blood pressure reduction over placebo to approve a new hypertension drug. Adverse effect rates exceeding 15% for any single side effect trigger a mandatory additional safety review. The total adverse effect rate (sum of all reported effects) must not exceed 30%.
questionStem: Does Drug ABX-200 meet the regulatory criteria for approval without triggering additional safety reviews?

options.text: Yes, it meets the efficacy threshold and does not trigger any safety reviews
Yes on efficacy, but it triggers a safety review due to the headache rate
No, it fails to meet the efficacy threshold
Yes on efficacy, but the total adverse effect rate triggers a safety review
No, it fails both the efficacy threshold and safety criteria

explanation: Efficacy: Treatment group reduction (18 mmHg) minus placebo (4 mmHg) = 14 mmHg improvement over placebo, exceeding the 10 mmHg threshold. Individual adverse effects: headache 12% < 15%, dizziness 8% < 15%, fatigue 5% < 15% — none trigger individual review. Total adverse rate: 12% + 8% + 5% = 25% < 30%. ABX-200 meets all criteria.

passage:
Source 1: Employee Benefits Summary
Cascade Corp offers two health insurance plans. Plan A: $250/month premium, $1,500 deductible, 80/20 coinsurance after deductible, $6,000 out-of-pocket maximum. Plan B: $400/month premium, $500 deductible, 90/10 coinsurance after deductible, $3,500 out-of-pocket maximum. Both plans cover preventive care at 100% with no deductible. Prescription drug copays: Plan A $30/prescription, Plan B $15/prescription.

Source 2: Employee Medical Usage Estimate
Employee Tom Harper expects the following medical expenses in 2025: Annual physical (preventive): $350. Two specialist visits: $200 each. One minor procedure: $4,000. Monthly prescription: 12 refills at retail price of $80 each. No emergency room visits expected.
questionStem: Under which plan would Tom Harper pay less in total annual out-of-pocket costs (premiums + deductible + coinsurance + copays)?

options.text: Both plans cost approximately the same

explanation: Plan A: Premiums: $250×12 = $3,000. Medical costs (non-preventive): $200+$200+$4,000 = $4,400. After $1,500 deductible, coinsurance = 20%×$2,900 = $580. Medical out-of-pocket: $2,080. Rx: 12×$30 = $360. Total: $5,440. Plan B: Premiums: $400×12 = $4,800. After $500 deductible, coinsurance = 10%×$3,900 = $390. Medical out-of-pocket: $890. Rx: 12×$15 = $180. Total: $5,870. Plan A saves approximately $430, roughly $500 less than Plan B.

Source 1: Private Equity Fund Terms
Crestview Capital Fund III has the following terms: Management fee: 2% of committed capital during the investment period (Years 1-5) and 2% of invested capital thereafter. Carried interest: 20% of profits above an 8% preferred return (compounded annually). GP commitment: 5% of total fund size. Fund size: $500 million. Investment period: 5 years. Fund life: 10 years.

Source 2: Portfolio Company Performance
The fund invested $300 million across 8 portfolio companies during the investment period. At the end of Year 10, total exit proceeds were $720 million. The fund returned all committed capital to LPs before calculating carried interest. Annual management fees totaled $80 million over the fund's life.
questionStem: Approximately how much carried interest did the GP earn?
explanation: Total exit proceeds: $720M. Total committed capital (returned to LPs): $500M. Net profit: $720M - $500M = $220M. In a simplified waterfall, the GP earns 20% carried interest on net profits above the preferred return. The 8% preferred return on $500M for the fund life creates a hurdle. However, using the common simplified GMAT approach: Carried interest = 20% × ($720M - $500M) = 20% × $220M = $44 million. Note: In practice, the preferred return hurdle would reduce this, but the question asks for an approximation using the given data.

passage:
Source 1: International Trade Regulations
Country X applies the following import tariffs: Raw materials: 5%. Semi-finished goods: 12%. Finished consumer goods: 25%. Goods from countries with a Free Trade Agreement (FTA) receive a 60% tariff reduction. An anti-dumping duty of 15% applies to steel products from Country Z regardless of FTA status. All imports are subject to a 10% value-added tax (VAT) calculated on the sum of the CIF value and any applicable tariffs.

Source 2: Import Shipment Details
Importer: GlobalMfg Corp (based in Country X)
Product: Steel beams (classified as semi-finished goods)
Origin: Country Z (has FTA with Country X)
CIF Value: $200,000
Shipment Weight: 50 metric tons
questionStem: What is the total amount of duties and taxes GlobalMfg must pay on this shipment?
explanation: Step 1: Base tariff for semi-finished goods: 12%. FTA 60% reduction: 12% × 0.40 = 4.8%. Tariff: $200,000 × 4.8% = $9,600. Step 2: Anti-dumping duty on steel from Country Z: 15% × $200,000 = $30,000 (applies regardless of FTA). Step 3: Total duties: $9,600 + $30,000 = $39,600. Step 4: VAT = 10% × (CIF + all duties) = 10% × ($200,000 + $39,600) = 10% × $239,600 = $23,960. Total payable: $39,600 + $23,960 = $63,560.

passage:
Source 1: Biotech Pipeline Summary
PharmaGen Inc. has three drugs in clinical trials: Drug A (Phase I, oncology), Drug B (Phase II, cardiology), Drug C (Phase III, neurology). Industry success rates: Phase I to approval: 10%. Phase II to approval: 25%. Phase III to approval: 60%. Expected peak annual revenue if approved: Drug A: $2 billion. Drug B: $800 million. Drug C: $1.5 billion. R&D costs remaining: Drug A: $400M. Drug B: $200M. Drug C: $100M.

Source 2: Valuation Methodology
Analysts value biotech pipelines using risk-adjusted net present value (rNPV). This method multiplies the expected revenue by the probability of success and applies a discount factor. For simplicity, assume a discount factor of 0.5 for all drugs and that peak revenue represents total expected commercial value. rNPV = (Peak Revenue × Probability of Success × Discount Factor) - Remaining R&D Costs.
questionStem: Based on the rNPV method described, which drug has the highest risk-adjusted value?

options.text: Drugs B and C have equal rNPV
All three drugs have negative rNPV

explanation: Drug A: rNPV = ($2B × 10% × 0.5) - $400M = $100M - $400M = -$300M. Drug B: rNPV = ($800M × 25% × 0.5) - $200M = $100M - $200M = -$100M. Drug C: rNPV = ($1.5B × 60% × 0.5) - $100M = $450M - $100M = $350M. Drug C has the highest rNPV at $350M. Note that Drugs A and B both have negative rNPV.

When evaluating a potential acquisition, Cascade Partners uses the following criteria: (1) Target's EBITDA margin must exceed 15%. (2) Revenue growth rate must be at least 8% annually over the past 3 years. (3) Debt-to-equity ratio must be below 2.0. (4) The acquisition price should not exceed 8× trailing EBITDA. (5) Target must operate in a market with projected 5%+ annual growth for the next 5 years.

Source 2: Target Company Profile
Company: DataStream Analytics
Revenue: 2021: $40M, 2022: $45M, 2023: $49M, 2024: $54M
EBITDA (2024): $9.2M
Total Debt: $18M
Total Equity: $12M
Asking Price: $70M
Market: Business intelligence software (projected 11% annual growth)

Source 3: Due Diligence Notes
DataStream's 2022 revenue spike included a one-time $3M licensing deal unlikely to recur. Adjusted 2022 revenue: $42M. Customer concentration risk: top 3 clients represent 45% of revenue.
questionStem: Using the adjusted figures, how many of Cascade Partners' five acquisition criteria does DataStream meet?
explanation: (1) EBITDA margin: $9.2M/$54M = 17.0% > 15% ✓. (2) Revenue growth 'at least 8% annually over the past 3 years' — checking each year with adjusted figures: 2021→2022: ($42M-$40M)/$40M = 5.0% ✗ (below 8%). 2022→2023: ($49M-$42M)/$42M = 16.7% ✓. 2023→2024: ($54M-$49M)/$49M = 10.2% ✓. Since 2022's adjusted growth was only 5%, criterion (2) is not met. (3) Debt-to-equity: $18M/$12M = 1.5 < 2.0 ✓. (4) Price/EBITDA: $70M/$9.2M ≈ 7.6× < 8× ✓. (5) Market growth: 11% > 5% ✓. DataStream meets 4 out of 5 criteria (fails criterion 2).

Route B (via Cape of Good Hope): Transit time 42 days, cost $2,600 per TEU, fuel surcharge $720/TEU, no canal fee. Both routes include insurance at 0.5% of cargo value. Port handling at both ends: $350/TEU.

Source 2: Shipment Details
Client: EuroTech Manufacturing
Cargo: 40 TEUs of electronic components
Declared Cargo Value: $2,000,000
Inventory Holding Cost: $150 per TEU per day
Urgency: Components needed for production starting in 35 days
questionStem: Considering all costs including inventory holding, which route is less expensive and by approximately how much?

options.text: Route A is cheaper by approximately $64,000
Route B is cheaper by approximately $50,000
Route A is cheaper by approximately $120,000
Route B is cheaper by approximately $30,000
Both routes cost approximately the same

explanation: Route A costs: Shipping (40×$3,200=$128,000) + Fuel (40×$450=$18,000) + Canal (40×$180=$7,200) + Port handling (40×$350=$14,000) + Insurance (0.5%×$2M=$10,000) + Holding (40×$150×28=$168,000) = $345,200. Route B costs: Shipping ($104,000) + Fuel ($28,800) + Port ($14,000) + Insurance ($10,000) + Holding (40×$150×42=$252,000) = $408,800. Route A is cheaper by $408,800 - $345,200 = $63,600 ≈ $64,000. Additionally, Route B's 42-day transit exceeds the 35-day deadline.

Tier 3 (Large farms, > 500 acres): $20/acre, maximum $15,000. Organic certification adds a 25% bonus. Farms in designated drought zones receive an additional $10/acre (not subject to tier maximums). Drought zone supplement: 350 × $10 = $3,500 (not subject to tier maximums). Total: $15,000 + $3,500 = $18,500. Farm has been operational since 2019 (6+ years), meeting the 3-year requirement.

passage:
Source 1: Climate Research Summary
A 10-year study of Lake Meridian found that average summer water temperatures increased from 18.2°C to 20.8°C. Dissolved oxygen levels declined from 8.5 mg/L to 6.2 mg/L. Algae bloom frequency increased from 2 events per summer to 7 events. Native trout population declined 40% while invasive carp population increased 120%. Phosphorus concentrations in the lake rose from 15 μg/L to 28 μg/L.

Source 2: Ecological Thresholds
Cold-water fish species (like trout) require dissolved oxygen above 7 mg/L and water temperatures below 20°C for optimal health. Algae blooms become likely when phosphorus exceeds 20 μg/L and water temperatures exceed 19°C. Carp thrive in warm, nutrient-rich waters and can tolerate dissolved oxygen as low as 3 mg/L. Lake ecosystem recovery requires reducing phosphorus below 18 μg/L and maintaining dissolved oxygen above 7.5 mg/L.
questionStem: Which of the following statements is best supported by both sources?

options.text: Reducing water temperature alone would restore the trout population
The increase in carp population directly caused the decline in trout population
Current lake conditions exceed multiple ecological thresholds, creating reinforcing negative effects on native species
Algae blooms are the primary cause of declining dissolved oxygen levels
The lake ecosystem can be restored by reducing phosphorus levels alone

explanation: Source 1 shows: temperature 20.8°C (above the 20°C threshold in Source 2), dissolved oxygen 6.2 mg/L (below the 7 mg/L threshold), phosphorus 28 μg/L (above the 20 μg/L threshold). Source 2 shows these thresholds are interconnected: high phosphorus + high temperature → algae blooms → lower oxygen → poor conditions for trout but favorable for carp. Multiple thresholds are exceeded simultaneously, creating compounding negative effects. A and E each address only one factor. B assumes direct causation not stated. D assumes causation not confirmed.

Source 1: Workforce Planning Data
TechForward Inc. currently employs 500 software engineers with an average salary of $130,000. Annual attrition rate: 18%. Cost to recruit and onboard one engineer: $40,000. During the 6-month ramp-up period, new hires operate at 50% productivity, costing the company an estimated $15,000 in lost output per hire.

Source 2: Retention Proposal
HR proposes a retention program costing $3,000 per employee per year (applied to all engineers). Industry data suggests such programs reduce attrition by 40%. The CFO has asked whether the program pays for itself within the first year.

Source 3: Market Conditions
The tech labor market is tightening. Average time-to-fill for engineering roles has increased to 60 days.
questionStem: Does the retention program pay for itself in the first year based on reduced attrition costs alone?

options.text: Yes, net savings are approximately $1.4 million
No, the program costs exceed savings by approximately $600,000
Yes, net savings are approximately $2.8 million
No, the program costs exceed savings by approximately $1.2 million

explanation: Current attrition: 18% × 500 = 90 engineers. Cost per departure: $40,000 (recruiting) + $15,000 (ramp-up) = $55,000. Total attrition cost: 90 × $55,000 = $4,950,000. With program: Attrition drops 40% to 10.8%, meaning 54 departures. New cost: 54 × $55,000 = $2,970,000. Savings: $4,950,000 - $2,970,000 = $1,980,000. Program cost: 500 × $3,000 = $1,500,000. Net savings: $1,980,000 - $1,500,000 = $480,000 ≈ $500,000. The program pays for itself.

explanation: Post-money valuation: $40M. Investor gets 25% ($10M/$40M). Option pool: 20% of post-money. Founders' share: 100% - 25% (investor) - 20% (option pool) = 55%. The option pool is carved from pre-money, effectively diluting founders before the investment. Founders go from 100% to 55% ownership.

Source 1: Transfer Pricing Regulations
Country A taxes corporate profits at 25%. Country B taxes at 15%. Under OECD guidelines, intercompany transactions must use arm's length pricing. If the transfer price exceeds 110% of the arm's length price, Country A may impose a 50% penalty on the excess. If the transfer price is below 90% of arm's length, Country B may impose a 30% penalty on the shortfall. Both countries have a mutual agreement procedure (MAP) that can take 18-24 months to resolve disputes.

Source 2: Transaction Details
MegaCorp's subsidiary in Country A manufactures widgets at a cost of $60/unit. It sells to MegaCorp's distribution subsidiary in Country B. Arm's length price (comparable transactions): $100/unit. MegaCorp sets transfer price at $80/unit. Annual volume: 500,000 units. Country B subsidiary sells to end customers at $150/unit.

Source 3: Tax Authority Audit
Country A's tax authority has initiated an audit, claiming the transfer price should be at least $95/unit. Country B's tax authority has not raised any objections to the current arrangement.
questionStem: If Country A adjusts the transfer price to $95/unit, what is the net tax impact on MegaCorp across both countries (assuming no MAP relief)?

options.text: No net change — tax paid in one country offsets the other

explanation: Current: Country A profit per unit = $80 - $60 = $20. Tax = $20 × 25% = $5/unit. Country B profit = $150 - $80 = $70. Tax = $70 × 15% = $10.50/unit. Total tax/unit: $15.50. Total for 500K units: $7,750,000. After adjustment to $95: Country A profit = $95 - $60 = $35. Tax = $35 × 25% = $8.75/unit. Country B: If Country B doesn't adjust, profit = $150 - $80 = $70 (still using original price for its records). Tax = $70 × 15% = $10.50/unit. New total tax/unit: $8.75 + $10.50 = $19.25. For 500K: $9,625,000. Increase: $9,625,000 - $7,750,000 = $1,875,000. This is the double taxation issue — Country A raises the price, but Country B doesn't grant a corresponding reduction without MAP.

If rates increase by 100 basis points: PBO decreases by approximately 12%. Equity impact: -5% to -8%. Fixed income: -6% to -8%.

questionStem: If interest rates decline by 100 basis points, what is the approximate new funded ratio of the Meridian pension fund?
explanation: Current funded ratio: $2.4B / $2.8B = 85.7%. If rates drop 100 bps: PBO increases 14%: $2.8B × 1.14 = $3.192B. Asset impact: Equities (60% of $2.4B = $1.44B) gain ~12.5% (midpoint): $1.44B × 1.125 = $1.62B. Fixed income (30% = $0.72B) gains ~9%: $0.72B × 1.09 = $0.785B. Alternatives (10% = $0.24B): assume minimal change, $0.24B. New assets: $1.62B + $0.785B + $0.24B = $2.645B. New funded ratio: $2.645B / $3.192B = 82.9% ≈ 80%. The funded ratio deteriorates despite asset gains because PBO growth outpaces asset growth.

Source 3: Risk Committee Mandate
The risk committee requires that maximum portfolio loss not exceed 3% in any 6-month period. If the forecasted loss exceeds this limit, duration must be reduced through hedging or rebalancing.
questionStem: What is the forecasted percentage price change, and does it breach the risk committee's loss limit?
explanation: Using the formula: ΔP/P = -D × Δy + ½ × C × (Δy)². D = 6.2, Δy = 0.0075 (75 bps), C = 52. First term: -6.2 × 0.0075 = -0.0465 = -4.65%. Second term: 0.5 × 52 × (0.0075)² = 26 × 0.00005625 = 0.001463 = +0.146%. Total: -4.65% + 0.146% = -4.504% ≈ -4.50%. This exceeds the 3% loss limit. Dollar impact: $500M × 4.50% = $22.5M loss. Action required: reduce portfolio duration through hedging.

Disputes are resolved through arbitration in Singapore. Exit clause: Either party may sell its stake after 3 years with 90-day notice; the other party has right of first refusal.

Source 2: JV Financial Performance (Year 3)
Total JV revenue: $45 million. Operating expenses: $28 million. Net profit: $17 million. AlphaTech independently licensed JV technology to a European firm for $5 million in revenue during Year 3. OmegaCorp is considering selling its 40% stake. An external buyer has offered $30 million.

Source 3: Valuation Context
Comparable AI JVs trade at 8-10× net profit. The JV's IP portfolio has been independently appraised at $60 million. OmegaCorp's 40% share of IP alone is valued at $24 million.
questionStem: What is OmegaCorp's total financial benefit from the JV in Year 3 (profit share + royalty income), and is the external offer of $30 million above or below the implied valuation range?

options.text: Year 3 benefit: $6.64M; $30M offer is within the valuation range
Year 3 benefit: $6.64M; $30M offer is below the valuation range
Year 3 benefit: $6.80M; $30M offer is below the valuation range
Year 3 benefit: $8.00M; $30M offer is within the valuation range
Year 3 benefit: $6.64M; $30M offer is above the valuation range

explanation: Management fee: 5% of profit = 5% × $17M = $850,000 (paid to AlphaTech). Distributable profit: $17M - $0.85M = $16.15M. OmegaCorp's 40%: $16.15M × 40% = $6.46M. AlphaTech's licensing royalty to JV: 8% × $5M = $400,000. OmegaCorp's 40% of royalty: $400K × 40% = $160K. Total OmegaCorp Year 3 benefit: $6.46M + $0.16M = $6.62M ≈ $6.64M (rounding). Valuation range: 8-10× net profit × 40% = (8×$17M to 10×$17M) × 40% = ($136M to $170M) × 40% = $54.4M to $68M. The $30M offer is significantly below this range. Even using just IP value, 40% = $24M < $30M, but the profit-multiple method gives much higher values.
